Content:
Alignment Unlocks Everything

When feasibility + direction + customer value are answered early, all three disciplines are energized

Culture is Made by Small Things

Cameras-on policy transformed engagement overnight
One person doing a daily icebreaker made one standup dramatically better than the other
Named teams ("The Custodians" for tech debt) create identity and pride
"Care about the person first" — learned the hard way that trying to prove yourself creates pushback
"Optimism solves more problems than anything"

Communication Is Key

As Product Managers drive business initiatives, communication is a common through-line for success. Clarity, aligning vision/strategy/execution & building trust with partners, accelerates how IC's and leads cross the finish line together as a team.

Good collaboration
clear runway to work in a focused and iterative way, while getting air cover from leads and stakeholders who will know how to speak to the value of the work across the org.
PD teams are usually spread thin and work in agile/hybrid methods out of necessity
Inefficient comms Can lead to mis-communication, or lower trust/transparency about how decisions get made,
Slack is more informal / casual compared to Teams
Slack as more customizable features with the emojis and different colors themes
Slack: Likes that they can show the time zone and can set up the status
Timezone related issue with teams in other cities
Open, friendly, helping culture makes the work easier

Bad collaboration
survival mode, with competing business priorities, unclear engineering, product and design constraints.
Smaller team allows for transparent communication
Never hesitate to put your point on the table

Working cross-functionally, when do you jump in?
When physical things need to get made to test a hypothesis

Individual contributor with most projects started by me

Working cross-functionally, when do you jump in?
Involves different stakeholders at each stage of a project

Across The EPD Workflow

The normal: Business priorities always change, clarity is never spoon-fed, alignment is always forged collectively
Good EPD Collaboration =  less errors + more user interaction & returning customers.
Teams are either Product or Engineering-led depending on the area of the business they're at (across consumers, innovation, and enterprise work-streams)
Repeat & Follow-up
Be firm on your decision

Ask right 
Questions

Collaboration Accelerators
GTM strategy for internal work-streams tools to repair fragmentation, build community, and help adoption

Collaboration Accelerators
Tools to track a lineage of a product space cohesively: PRDs, GTM strategies, Prototypes, Roadmaps, Technical/Design Specs

Small to mid-sized companies check-in across their EPD counterparts informally and often, working closely together to get a gut check on what everyone is up to to stay tightly aligned. There's also emphasis on team dynamics, culture and bonding, with more flexibility to grow into senior or adjacent roles longer term

Large companies have endless channels of communication, with a tendency of siloed work that can fragment and disrupt opportunity to build centralized workflow practices if innovation is prioritized over long-term ecosystem health (tech debt, maintenance work, quality rubrics)
Cross-functional meetings help make decision-making faster and easier

AI
PM: Having agents and being able to prototype and code will mean having less headcount, but it also means less time wasted on communication to align and build trust

AI
Engineering: Great for basic tasks, but can get concerning if a prototype is considered for production/implemented within a technical stack.  There’s no plug-and-play way to integrate a generated tool into a mature tech

AI
Design: designer role is blurred when PM’s and Engineers can generate prototypes.  The tooling isn’t as good as it could be to replace designers outright, but it will definitely have companies pressure design to do more with less, as junior positions begin to fade.

How do decisions get made?
Mix of inputs from all stakeholders and the limitations of the scope

Designers design the feature and get it finalized by leadership.

How do decisions get made?
Engineering team decides on the technical implementation.

How do decisions get made?
As a UX designer, I make the most of decisions for the user flows and UI solutions. Then the team and clients will provide feedback and comments. I'll iterate the design based on that.

Surfacing Assumptions & Alignment

*Everyone Mentioned this across 5 interviews

Early Cross-Functional Input

Information Overload & Context Chasing

Biggest Challenge: Scope of our technical problems need direction often from the business side, even if the technical feasibility/design can be done. It’s not designing things well, but designing what.
A Designer with strong perspective and asking the question”Is it really necessary?” bridges the gap between all the teams and in turn benefits the workflow and user engagement
As a PM you need to keep repeating your projects to get better insights on the purpose, end goal, the WHY.

Tips/Tricks for Collaboration:
If it's a PM-driven pod, you should ask early about how the PM prefers to work with PD, and if you're not aligned with their POV, explain why
Ask for optimizations to the ways of working that help you contribute to the process where you think your role is critical.
Explain why you think that's critical, not in a combative way, but in a way that offers a POV for how you think you can make the overall work better, and also improve the overall working dynamic.
Distribute decision-making to partners who know their stuff while course-correcting where it counts

use AI to summarize info

Potential problem statement: EPD teams lack a shared source of truth that evolves with the project, forcing them to rely on meetings and tribal knowledge to stay aligned, which breaks down across time zones, seniority gaps, and shifting requirements.
